skill.name: pathml
skill.description: A full-featured computational pathology toolkit for advanced WSI analysis, including multiplexed immunofluorescence (CODEX, Vectra), nuclei segmentation, tissue graph construction, and machine learning model training on pathology data. Supports over 160 slide formats. For simple tile extraction from H&E slides, histolab may be simpler.
skill.summary: Computational pathology toolkit for whole slide image analysis with PathML
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 579 static findings are false positives located in documentation markdown files (references/*.md and SKILL.md), not in executable code. The detected patterns include PyTorch model.eval() calls misidentified as dynamic code evaluation, markdown code fence backticks misinterpreted as shell execution, mention of hash algorithms in documentation flagged as weak cryptography, and legitimate resource URLs in reference sections. This is an instruction-only workflow skill with no packaged executable scripts. All prior audit veto gates (stability, contract, determinism, security, scientific integrity) passed evaluation.

content.value_statement: PathML is a comprehensive toolkit for computational pathology and whole slide image analysis. This skill gives AI assistants expert guidance on using PathML capabilities including slide loading, preprocessing, nuclei segmentation, graph construction, and model training.
actual_capabilities: Load whole slide images from over 160 proprietary formats using PathML slide classes
Build preprocessing pipelines with stain normalization, tissue detection, and quality control
Perform nuclei detection, segmentation, and feature extraction for downstream analysis
Construct cell and tissue graphs for spatial analysis and graph neural network workflows
Train and deploy deep learning models (HoVer-Net, HACTNet) for pathology data
Analyze multiplexed immunofluorescence data including CODEX and Vectra platforms
limitations: Requires external installation of the PathML Python package and all dependencies
Not designed for real-time clinical diagnosis or medical decision-making
Performance depends on available hardware, GPU recommended for model training
Reference documentation covers common PathML usage but may not cover every edge case

use_cases.title: Analyze H&E stained tissue sections
use_cases.description: Pathologists and researchers can load whole slide images, apply stain normalization, detect tissue regions, and segment nuclei for quantitative analysis.

use_cases.title: Train nuclei segmentation models
use_cases.description: Machine learning engineers can use PathML integrated PyTorch pipelines to train HoVer-Net models on pathology datasets with pre-built data loaders.

use_cases.title: Process multiplexed immunofluorescence data
use_cases.description: Researchers working with CODEX or Vectra platforms can collapse multi-cycle data, segment cells using Mesmer, and quantify marker expression for spatial proteomics.

prompt_templates.title: Load and visualize a whole slide image
prompt_templates.prompt: Load the whole slide image at [path/to/slide.svs] using PathML SlideData. Generate tiles at level 1 with tile size 256 and stride 256. Show the first 5 tiles and their dimensions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with WSI loading in PathML

prompt_templates.title: Build a preprocessing pipeline for H&E slides
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PathML preprocessing pipeline for H&E slides. Include tissue detection, Macenko stain normalization, and median blur transforms. Apply the pipeline to [slide_path] and save the result to HDF5 format.

prompt_templates.title: Train HoVer-Net for nuclei segmentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a training workflow using PathML to train a HoVer-Net model for nuclei segmentation. Use the CoNSeP dataset for training. Include data loaders, loss functions, and evaluation metrics.
prompt_templates.scenario: Deep learning model training for pathology

prompt_templates.title: Analyze CODEX multiplexed imaging data
prompt_templates.prompt: Load a CODEX slide using PathML CODEXSlide class. Collapse multi-cycle runs at z-slice 2. Segment cells using Mesmer with DAPI as nuclear channel. Quantify CD3, CD4, CD8, and CD20 marker expression. Export results to AnnData format.

output_examples.input: What are the steps to analyze an H&E stained tissue slide with PathML?
output: The analysis follows five main stages. First, load the whole slide image using SlideData.from_slide(). Second, generate tiles at an appropriate level and tile size. Third, build a preprocessing pipeline with tissue detection and stain normalization. Fourth, apply the pipeline to segment nuclei and extract features. Fifth, save the processed data to HDF5 format for downstream analysis. The SKILL.md file provides a complete example workflow.

output_examples.input: How do I train a nuclei segmentation model with PathML?
output: PathML integrates with PyTorch for model training. Start by preparing your pathology dataset using the SlideDataset class. Then create PyTorch DataLoaders using PathML TileDataset wrappers. The library includes pre-built models like HoVer-Net for simultaneous nuclei segmentation and classification. Training uses standard PyTorch loops with specialized loss functions for pathology tasks. After training, export models to ONNX format for production deployment.

best_practices: Always verify slide format compatibility before loading, as some formats may need additional dependencies.
Use appropriate tile size and stride values based on your analysis goal, small tiles for nuclei and large tiles for tissue patterns.
Save intermediate results to HDF5 format regularly to enable efficient random access during iterative analysis.
anti_patterns: Loading entire slide datasets into memory instead of using memory-mapped HDF5 access for large pathology datasets.
Applying identical preprocessing pipelines to H&E and multiplexed immunofluorescence data without adjusting for stain differences.
Training models without stain normalization preprocessing, which introduces batch effects across slide sources.

faq.question: What slide formats does PathML support?
faq.answer: PathML supports over 160 proprietary pathology slide formats including Aperio SVS, Hamamatsu NDPI, Leica SCN, Zeiss ZVI, DICOM, and OME-TIFF.

faq.question: Do I need a GPU to use PathML?
faq.answer: A GPU is recommended for training deep learning models like HoVer-Net. Basic preprocessing and tile extraction can run on CPU.

faq.question: How does PathML handle large whole slide images?
faq.answer: PathML uses tile-based processing and HDF5 storage with memory-mapped access to handle large WSI files without loading entire slides into memory.

faq.question: Can I use PathML with my own deep learning models?
faq.answer: Yes, PathML integrates with PyTorch so you can use its data loading and preprocessing with custom model architectures.

faq.question: What is the difference between PathML and histolab?
faq.answer: PathML is a full toolkit for advanced WSI analysis including multiplexed immunofluorescence and model training. Histolab is simpler and better for basic tile extraction from H&E slides.

faq.question: How do I install PathML?
faq.answer: Install PathML using pip or uv: uv pip install pathml. For full functionality including ML features, use uv pip install pathml[all].
